texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: REMOVAL OF  ORGANIC VAPORS FROM  AIR  BY  SELECTIVE MEMBRANE PERMEATION*
texts.text: REMOVAL OF  ORGANIC VAPORS FROM  AIR  BY  SELECTIVE MEMBRANE PERMEATION*

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fraunhofer-Institut fiir  Grenzfltichen-  und  Bioverfahrenstechnik, Nobelstrasse 12, 7000 Stuttgart  80  (F.R.G.)
texts.text: Fraunhofer-Institut fiir  Grenzfltichen-  und  Bioverfahrenstechnik, Nobelstrasse 12, 7000 Stuttgart  80  (F.R.G.)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (Received  October 17,1986;  accepted in revised form February 18,1987)
texts.text: (Received  October 17,1986;  accepted in revised form February 18,1987)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Many industrial processes such as printing, metal cleaning or painting produce waste air streams containing low concentrations  of organic solvents, such as acetone, toluene, perchloroethane, xylene, etc. The total value of the solvent lost with the waste air is considerable. In addition,  these solvents represent a significant pollution  problem, and in recent years several procedures for recovering solvents from air, such as carbon adsorption, incineration,  etc. have been introduced in the industry. All of them show some draw-backs in terms of efficiency,  reliability and costs.  In this paper a membrane process is described, which provides an attractive alternative to the conventional methods. In a basic study the permeabilities of acetone, toluene, xylene, dichloroethane and dichloromethane  through a homogeneous  polydimethylsiloxane  (PDMS) membrane have been determined using a pressure difference between 400 and  1000 mbar as driving force. The selectivity of the membrane for the various solvents and their dependence on the solvent concentration in a mixture with nitrogen were studied. The selectivities of the PDMS  membrane for the solvent/nitrogen  mixtures were in the range of S=70-160, depending on the solvent and its concentration  in  the feed mixture. Based on these data a pilot  plant process has been designed in which a solvent is recovered from a waste air stream and the depleted air is recycled into the waste air producing process. The solvent which has permeated the membrane is recovered as a liquid by condensation.  Thus,  complete  recycling of air and solvents is possible.  In a cost analysis it has been demonstrated that the membrane process is indeed an attractive alternative to conventional air cleaning techniques.
texts.text: Many industrial processes such as printing, metal cleaning or painting produce waste air streams containing low concentrations  of organic solvents, such as acetone, toluene, perchloroethane, xylene, etc. The total value of the solvent lost with the waste air is considerable. In addition,  these solvents represent a significant pollution  problem, and in recent years several procedures for recovering solvents from air, such as carbon adsorption, incineration,  etc. have been introduced in the industry. All of them show some draw-backs in terms of efficiency,  reliability and costs.  In this paper a membrane process is described, which provides an attractive alternative to the conventional methods. In a basic study the permeabilities of acetone, toluene, xylene, dichloroethane and dichloromethane  through a homogeneous  polydimethylsiloxane  (PDMS) membrane have been determined using a pressure difference between 400 and  1000 mbar as driving force. The selectivity of the membrane for the various solvents and their dependence on the solvent concentration in a mixture with nitrogen were studied. The selectivities of the PDMS  membrane for the solvent/nitrogen  mixtures were in the range of S=70-160, depending on the solvent and its concentration  in  the feed mixture. Based on these data a pilot  plant process has been designed in which a solvent is recovered from a waste air stream and the depleted air is recycled into the waste air producing process. The solvent which has permeated the membrane is recovered as a liquid by condensation.  Thus,  complete  recycling of air and solvents is possible.  In a cost analysis it has been demonstrated that the membrane process is indeed an attractive alternative to conventional air cleaning techniques.

texts.label: text

texts.orig: crofiltration  nowadays  are industrial processes with substantial technical  and commercial  significance.  More  recently  synthetic  membranes  have also been utilized for  the  separation  of  various  gas mixtures.  The  recovery  of  hydrogen from petrochemical  purge gas streams or ammonia  production  plants and the enrichment  of  oxygen  or  nitrogen  from  air are two  areas where  membranes have established themselves  as very useful tools.
texts.text: crofiltration  nowadays  are industrial processes with substantial technical  and commercial  significance.  More  recently  synthetic  membranes  have also been utilized for  the  separation  of  various  gas mixtures.  The  recovery  of  hydrogen from petrochemical  purge gas streams or ammonia  production  plants and the enrichment  of  oxygen  or  nitrogen  from  air are two  areas where  membranes have established themselves  as very useful tools.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since  membranes  can be tailored to fit a certain mass separation task, separation problems which are difficult to handle by conventional  procedures may be  treatable  by  a membrane  process.  One  of  these  problems,  the  removal  of organic  solvents  from waste air streams, is discussed in this paper.
texts.text: Since  membranes  can be tailored to fit a certain mass separation task, separation problems which are difficult to handle by conventional  procedures may be  treatable  by  a membrane  process.  One  of  these  problems,  the  removal  of organic  solvents  from waste air streams, is discussed in this paper.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  industrial  processes  such  as painting,  printing,  dry  cleaning  and  metal processing  large amounts  of solvents  such as toluene,  xylene,  dichloroethane, trichloroethane  and acetone  are emitted into the atmosphere.  These  solvents are not  only  a sever environmental  hazard but also present  a significant  economical  loss. Most  traditional  methods  to recover these solvents,  such as carbon  adsorption,  have so far proved  to be unsatisfactory.  Since the carbon  has to be generated with hot  steam, recycling  of solvents  is often  problematic  because  of  cracking  of  organic  molecules,  and  corrosion  problems  may  be  encountered  when chlorine  is obtained  from chlorinate  hydrocarbons  during the steam regenerating process.
texts.text: In  industrial  processes  such  as painting,  printing,  dry  cleaning  and  metal processing  large amounts  of solvents  such as toluene,  xylene,  dichloroethane, trichloroethane  and acetone  are emitted into the atmosphere.  These  solvents are not  only  a sever environmental  hazard but also present  a significant  economical  loss. Most  traditional  methods  to recover these solvents,  such as carbon  adsorption,  have so far proved  to be unsatisfactory.  Since the carbon  has to be generated with hot  steam, recycling  of solvents  is often  problematic  because  of  cracking  of  organic  molecules,  and  corrosion  problems  may  be  encountered  when chlorine  is obtained  from chlorinate  hydrocarbons  during the steam regenerating process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  separation  and  recovery  of  organic  vapors  from  waste air streams by selective  membrane  permeation  seems to  be  an  attractive  alternative  to  the conventional  process, with the following  significant  features:
texts.text: The  separation  and  recovery  of  organic  vapors  from  waste air streams by selective  membrane  permeation  seems to  be  an  attractive  alternative  to  the conventional  process, with the following  significant  features:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Direct recovery  of organic  solvent  without  loss.
texts.text: Direct recovery  of organic  solvent  without  loss.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. Low process  cost due to solvent  recovery  and intrinsic process parameters.
texts.text: Low process  cost due to solvent  recovery  and intrinsic process parameters.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For membrane  processes  to be useful, membranes  are required that  have a much  higher  permeability  to  organic  solvents  than  to  air. These  membranes have to be installed into suitable units, i.e., a module, and the separation process itself has to be adjusted to the special requirements of the problem.  In this paper the development  of a process  suited for the recovery  of organic  solvents from industrial waste air streams is described.
texts.text: For membrane  processes  to be useful, membranes  are required that  have a much  higher  permeability  to  organic  solvents  than  to  air. These  membranes have to be installed into suitable units, i.e., a module, and the separation process itself has to be adjusted to the special requirements of the problem.  In this paper the development  of a process  suited for the recovery  of organic  solvents from industrial waste air streams is described.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Based of results of pilot plant tests a cost estimate is carried out for a painting operation  waste air membrane  treatment system.
texts.text: Based of results of pilot plant tests a cost estimate is carried out for a painting operation  waste air membrane  treatment system.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  process  to  recover  organic  solvents  from  waste air streams from  a solvent-carrying  operation  such as painting  or metal cleaning  is shown schematically in the flow diagram of Fig. 1. The solvent-laden  exhaust air stream exits a solvent  drying  chamber  and is passed through  a membrane  module. The  organic vapors pass through  the membrane.  The  solvent-depleted  air is recycled
texts.text: The  process  to  recover  organic  solvents  from  waste air streams from  a solvent-carrying  operation  such as painting  or metal cleaning  is shown schematically in the flow diagram of Fig. 1. The solvent-laden  exhaust air stream exits a solvent  drying  chamber  and is passed through  a membrane  module. The  organic vapors pass through  the membrane.  The  solvent-depleted  air is recycled

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 1. Flow diagram of a membrane solvent recovery system.
texts.text: Fig. 1. Flow diagram of a membrane solvent recovery system.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: to the  chamber  where  it is again  loaded with  organic  solvent  vapors,  providing a more  or less  closed  system  with  the  membrane  functioning as a 'kidney'  for the  continuous  removal  of solvent  vapors.  A vacuum pump on the  downstream side  of  the  membrane provides  the  necessary driving  force  for  the  solvent transmembrane flux.  The  solvent  vapors  are  condensed  in  a heat  exchanger and recovered  as liquid.
texts.text: to the  chamber  where  it is again  loaded with  organic  solvent  vapors,  providing a more  or less  closed  system  with  the  membrane  functioning as a 'kidney'  for the  continuous  removal  of solvent  vapors.  A vacuum pump on the  downstream side  of  the  membrane provides  the  necessary driving  force  for  the  solvent transmembrane flux.  The  solvent  vapors  are  condensed  in  a heat  exchanger and recovered  as liquid.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2 Transport parameters of membranes in gas separation
texts.text: 2.2 Transport parameters of membranes in gas separation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  key  component in  any  membrane-based gas  separation system  is  the polymer  of  which  the  membrane is  made.  This polymer  should  have  a  high permeability  and good selectivity  for the  solvents  to be recovered. Also, it should be easily  formed  into  a suitable  membrane  structure  and  an economical  membrane  module.  The  development  of a hollow fiber  composite  membrane  with  a 1.0 pm polydimethylsiloxane selective  barrier  supported  on  a porous  polysulfone  substructure, which  seems  particularly suited  for the  recovery  of organic vapors,  is  described  elsewhere [ 11. This  type  of  membrane has  been  used  in all tests  carried  out  in this  study.
texts.text: The  key  component in  any  membrane-based gas  separation system  is  the polymer  of  which  the  membrane is  made.  This polymer  should  have  a  high permeability  and good selectivity  for the  solvents  to be recovered. Also, it should be easily  formed  into  a suitable  membrane  structure  and  an economical  membrane  module.  The  development  of a hollow fiber  composite  membrane  with  a 1.0 pm polydimethylsiloxane selective  barrier  supported  on  a porous  polysulfone  substructure, which  seems  particularly suited  for the  recovery  of organic vapors,  is  described  elsewhere [ 11. This  type  of  membrane has  been  used  in all tests  carried  out  in this  study.

texts.text: Here, Ji is the  gas  flux,  Di the  diffusion  coefficient of  the  component i in  the polymer  matrix,  ki its distribution coefficient between  the  gas and the  polymer phase, Ap the  difference in  its  partial  pressure  in the  two phases  separated  by the  membrane,  and Ax the  thickness of the  polymer  barrier.

texts.orig: For reasons of convenience  the product  of Di and ki is in the literature often referred to as permeability:
texts.text: For reasons of convenience  the product  of Di and ki is in the literature often referred to as permeability:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It has, however,  to be kept in mind, that the very simple relation  expressed in eqn.  (1)  holds  true only  for  ideal gases. For organic  vapors both  the diffusion coefficient Di as well as the distribution  coefficient  are a function  of the composition  of  the  gas phase.  Usually,  the  difference  and the  distribution  coefficient  of  an  organic  vapor  increases  drastically  with  an  increase  in  its initial pressure in the gas phase.
texts.text: It has, however,  to be kept in mind, that the very simple relation  expressed in eqn.  (1)  holds  true only  for  ideal gases. For organic  vapors both  the diffusion coefficient Di as well as the distribution  coefficient  are a function  of the composition  of  the  gas phase.  Usually,  the  difference  and the  distribution  coefficient  of  an  organic  vapor  increases  drastically  with  an  increase  in  its initial pressure in the gas phase.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All tests were performed  with a polydimethylsiloxane membrane  as the selective barrier. The  permeabilities  and selectivities  of this membrane  for various vapors  and gases were determined  with simple components  using a wellstirred batch  cell and flat membrane  sheets. Pilot plant experiments  were carried out with hollow fiber composite  membranes, simulating a solvent-carrying waste air stream.
texts.text: All tests were performed  with a polydimethylsiloxane membrane  as the selective barrier. The  permeabilities  and selectivities  of this membrane  for various vapors  and gases were determined  with simple components  using a wellstirred batch  cell and flat membrane  sheets. Pilot plant experiments  were carried out with hollow fiber composite  membranes, simulating a solvent-carrying waste air stream.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While the separation of organic solvent vapors from waste air streams seems to be technically  feasible there is still the question  of economics.  Based on the data obtained  in the experimental  part of this study, a preliminary  cost  estimate provides  some  information  about  the  economics  of  the  process.  As  an example  of a practical  application  the costs  for the removal  of an organic  solvent from the waste air stream of a painting  process  drying chamber  are estimated. The  cost calculation  is based on the following  process parameters:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The costs calculated  for the practical  studies application  are estimated to be DM 0.56/kg recovered solvent. In this case the recovered solvent by itself makes the process economically  feasible. It has, however, to be kept in mind that  (1) complete  removal  of  the  solvent  is  not  possible  or  requires  extremely  large membrane areas and  (2)  the amount of solvent recovered by a membrane unit of  certain  size depends  very  much  on  the  solvent  vapor  concentration in the exhaust  air stream. The  most favorable  case is achieved  when the exhaust air has a high solvent  vapor  concentration  and is recycled  to the drying chamber only partially  depleted  of solvent.
texts.text: The costs calculated  for the practical  studies application  are estimated to be DM 0.56/kg recovered solvent. In this case the recovered solvent by itself makes the process economically  feasible. It has, however, to be kept in mind that  (1) complete  removal  of  the  solvent  is  not  possible  or  requires  extremely  large membrane areas and  (2)  the amount of solvent recovered by a membrane unit of  certain  size depends  very  much  on  the  solvent  vapor  concentration in the exhaust  air stream. The  most favorable  case is achieved  when the exhaust air has a high solvent  vapor  concentration  and is recycled  to the drying chamber only partially  depleted  of solvent.
